items.title: Email spam filter
items.scenario: Training data: 50,000 emails, each labeled spam or not-spam. Features might include word counts, sender domain, and link density. The model learns f(email features) → {spam, ham} and flags new incoming mail.
items.explanation: Classic binary classification under supervision: every training email has a human- or rule-assigned label y that defines the training objective.

items.title: House price prediction
items.scenario: Each row is a home with features (sq ft, bedrooms, zip code) and label y = sale price in dollars. Linear regression or gradient boosting fits ŷ ≈ price; a new listing's features produce a predicted price.
items.explanation: Regression is supervised when the target is a continuous numeric label collected from past sales.

items.title: Handwritten digit recognition (MNIST)
items.scenario: 28×28 pixel images of digits 0–9, each image labeled with the correct digit. A neural network maps pixel vector x to class y ∈ {0,…,9}; after training, it classifies scanned digits the model never saw.
items.explanation: Multi-class classification with one label per image — the canonical classroom example of supervised learning.

items.title: Medical image screening
items.scenario: Radiologists label 10,000 chest X-rays as normal or pneumonia. A CNN trains on (image, label) pairs; at inference it scores new scans for triage. Labels are costly and may be reviewed by multiple experts.
items.explanation: Shows why supervised learning depends on label quality and quantity — errors in y directly teach the wrong mapping.

items.title: Instruction tuning for chatbots
items.scenario: A dataset of (user prompt, ideal assistant response) pairs fine-tunes a language model. Each pair is a supervised example: input x is the prompt, target y is the reference response (or token sequence).
items.explanation: Modern Gen AI still uses the supervised paradigm when human-written or curated (prompt, response) labels drive training.
